summary: ByteDance Seed introduced Seed Full-Duplex Speech LLM, a new voice model designed for real-time, interruption-tolerant conversation with attentive listening and interference suppression, alongside a Dola-Seed-2.0-Preview release on Arena. Google expanded its AI-powered Google Finance experience to Europe, extending generative financial search and analysis into a major new market. On the research side, the paper "Tool Calling is Linearly Readable and Steerable in Language Models" reports that tool selection signals are linearly decodable across 12 instruction-tuned models from 270M to 27B parameters, pointing to a potentially practical control layer for agent reliability.

The announcement came directly from ByteDance Seed, making this an official product artifact rather than a third-party leak or benchmark claim.

The artifact is a live product expansion from Google AI Blog, not a concept demo, indicating operational confidence in the feature set.
Because Google Finance sits inside a broader Google ecosystem, the European launch strengthens Google's position in consumer financial discovery against standalone finance and data platforms.

top_topics.title: Tool Calling is Linearly Readable and Steerable in Language Models
top_topics.summary: When a tool-calling agent picks the wrong tool, the failure is invisible until execution: the email gets sent, the meeting gets missed. Probing 12 instruction-tuned models across Gemma 3, Qwen 3, Qwen 2.5, and Llama 3.1 (270M to 27B), we find the identity of the chosen tool is linearly readable and steerable inside the model. Adding the mean-difference between two tools' average internal activations switches which tool the model selects at 77-100% accuracy on name-only single-turn prompts (93-100% at 4B+), and the JSON arguments that follow autoregressively match the new tool's schema, so f...

items.title: Tool Calling is Linearly Readable and Steerable in Language Models
items.summary: When a tool-calling agent picks the wrong tool, the failure is invisible until execution: the email gets sent, the meeting gets missed. Probing 12 instruction-tuned models across Gemma 3, Qwen 3, Qwen 2.5, and Llama 3.1 (270M to 27B), we find the identity of the chosen tool is linearly readable and steerable inside the model. Adding the mean-difference between two tools' average internal activations switches which tool the model selects at 77-100% accuracy on name-only single-turn prompts (93-100% at 4B+), and the JSON arguments that follow autoregressively match the new tool's schema, so f...

metadata.comment: Finds tool identity is linearly readable and steerable in internal activations, with causal localization to output-direction and specific heads.

top_topics.SHORT_COMMENT: When a tool-calling agent picks the wrong tool, the failure is invisible until execution: the email gets sent, the meeting gets missed. Probing 12 instruction-tuned models across Gemma 3, Qwen 3, Qwen 2.5, and Llama 3.1 (270M to 27B), we find the identity of the chosen tool is linearly readable and steerable inside the model. Adding the mean-difference between two tools' average internal activations switches which tool the model selects at 77-100% accuracy on name-only single-turn prompts (93-100% at 4B+), and the JSON arguments that follow autoregressively match the new tool's schema, so f...
top_topics.WHY_IT_MATTERS: If tool selection can be read and steered with lightweight probes, enterprises may gain a lower-cost path to improving agent reliability without retraining frontier models.
top_topics.HEADLINE: "Tool Calling is Linearly Readable and Steerable in Language Models" Finds Decodable Tool-Choice Signals Across 12 Models
KEY_TAKEAWAYS: The paper studies 12 instruction-tuned models spanning Gemma 3, Qwen 3, Qwen 2.5, and Llama 3.1, with parameter sizes ranging from 270M to 27B.
Authors report that the identity of the chosen tool is linearly readable from model representations, implying tool-use decisions are encoded in a relatively accessible form before execution.
The work also claims steerability, suggesting interventions on internal representations can shift tool choice and potentially reduce silent agent failures such as wrong API calls or missed actions.

topics.title: Where's the Plan? Locating Latent Planning in Language Models with Lightweight Mechanistic Interventions
topics.summary: We study planning site formation in language models -- where internal representations of structurally-constrained future tokens form during the forward pass, and whether they causally drive generation. Using rhyming-couplet completion as a clean test of forward-looking constraint, we apply two lightweight methods (linear probing and activation patching) across Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3 at more than ten scales. Probing shows that future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at the line boundary, with signal that strengthens with scale in all three families. Activation patching reveals th...

items.title: Where's the Plan? Locating Latent Planning in Language Models with Lightweight Mechanistic Interventions
items.summary: We study planning site formation in language models -- where internal representations of structurally-constrained future tokens form during the forward pass, and whether they causally drive generation. Probing shows that future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at the line boundary, with signal that strengthens with scale in all three families. Activation patching reveals th...

metadata.comment: Localizes where future-token planning representations form and causally matter during generation using probing and activation/path patching.

topics.title: Beyond LoRA vs. Full Fine-Tuning: Gradient-Guided Optimizer Routing for LLM Adaptation
topics.summary: Recent literature on fine-tuning Large Language Models highlights a fundamental debate. While Full Fine-Tuning (FFT) provides the representational plasticity required for high-entropy knowledge injection, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) can match or surpass FFT performance because many tasks only require updates in a low-rank space and benefit from LoRA's additional regularization. Through empirical evaluation across diverse tasks (SQL, Medical QA, and Counterfactual Knowledge) and varying language models (Gemma-3-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, and Qwen2.5-3B), we verify both trends and demonstrate that rely...

items.title: Beyond LoRA vs. Full Fine-Tuning: Gradient-Guided Optimizer Routing for LLM Adaptation
items.summary: Recent literature on fine-tuning Large Language Models highlights a fundamental debate. Through empirical evaluation across diverse tasks (SQL, Medical QA, and Counterfactual Knowledge) and varying language models (Gemma-3-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, and Qwen2.5-3B), we verify both trends and demonstrate that rely...

metadata.comment: Dynamically routes adaptation updates between full fine-tuning and LoRA using optimizer-level gradient access to both experts.

topics.title: Future Validity is the Missing Statistic: From Impossibility to $\Phi$-Estimation for Grammar-Faithful Speculative Decoding
topics.summary: Grammar-constrained generation is often combined with local vocabulary masking and speculative decoding, but the resulting sampling law is not the grammar-conditional distribution users usually intend. We show that any speculative decoder with local mask access, Leviathan rejection, and rollback soundness samples from the locally projected distribution $\mu^{\mathrm{proj}}$ rather than the grammar-conditional distribution $\mu^\star$. This extends the GAD impossibility result to speculative decoding; on Dyck grammars with Qwen3-8B, the total-variation gap can reach 0.996. We identify the fu...

items.title: Future Validity is the Missing Statistic: From Impossibility to $\Phi$-Estimation for Grammar-Faithful Speculative Decoding
items.summary: Grammar-constrained generation is often combined with local vocabulary masking and speculative decoding, but the resulting sampling law is not the grammar-conditional distribution users usually intend. We show that any speculative decoder with local mask access, Leviathan rejection, and rollback soundness samples from the locally projected distribution $\mu^{\mathrm{proj}}$ rather than the grammar-conditional distribution $\mu^\star$. This extends the GAD impossibility result to speculative decoding; on Dyck grammars with Qwen3-8B, the total-variation gap can reach 0.996. We identify the fu...

metadata.comment: Shows local-mask speculative decoding cannot recover grammar-conditional sampling and identifies future validity as the missing statistic for exact correction.

topics.title: The Coupling Tax: How Shared Token Budgets Undermine Visible Chain-of-Thought Under Fixed Output Limits
topics.summary: Chain-of-thought reasoning is often treated as a monotone way to improve language-model accuracy by letting a model think longer. We identify a countervailing effect, the coupling tax: when reasoning traces and final answers share one output-token budget, long traces can crowd out the answer they are meant to support. Across GSM8K, MATH-500, and five BIG-Bench Hard tasks with Qwen3 models at three scales, non-thinking mode matches or outperforms thinking mode on GSM8K and MATH-500 at every budget up to 2048 tokens, while harder tasks shift the crossover to larger budgets. We derive a trunca...

We derive a trunca...

metadata.comment: Identifies a token-budget coupling mechanism that makes visible chain-of-thought hurt accuracy and proposes split-budget decoding.

topics.title: A Rod Flow Model for Adam at the Edge of Stability
topics.summary: Cohen et al. (arXiv:2207.14484) observed that adaptive gradient methods such as Adam operate at the edge of stability. While there has been significant work on continuous-time modeling of gradient descent at the edge of stability, extending these models to momentum methods remains underdeveloped. In the gradient descent setting, Regis et al. (arXiv:2602.01480) introduced rod flow, which models consecutive iterates as an extended one-dimensional object -- a "rod." Here we extend rod flow to Adam by working in the joint phase space of parameters and first moment $(w, m)$ and treating the seco...

metadata.comment: Extends rod-flow continuous-time modeling to Adam and other momentum optimizers at the edge of stability.

topics.SHORT_COMMENT: Cohen et al. (arXiv:2207.14484) observed that adaptive gradient methods such as Adam operate at the edge of stability. While there has been significant work on continuous-time modeling of gradient descent at the edge of stability, extending these models to momentum methods remains underdeveloped. In the gradient descent setting, Regis et al. (arXiv:2602.01480) introduced rod flow, which models consecutive iterates as an extended one-dimensional object -- a "rod." Here we extend rod flow to Adam by working in the joint phase space of parameters and first moment $(w, m)$ and treating the seco...

paper_spotlight.label: New Frontier Papers
paper_spotlight.description: Papers that appear to open a genuinely new direction, paradigm, or field.

items.title: Spectral Dynamics in Deep Networks: Feature Learning, Outlier Escape, and Learning Rate Transfer
items.summary: Broadly important today and potentially frontier-opening: Develops DMFT for bulk and outlier spectral dynamics in deep nets, linking width scaling to feature learning and learning-rate transfer.
items.spotlight_comment: Broadly important today and potentially frontier-opening: Develops DMFT for bulk and outlier spectral dynamics in deep nets, linking width scaling to feature learning and learning-rate transfer.

items.title: Self-Consolidating Language Models: Continual Knowledge Incorporation from Context
items.summary: The paper proposes an unusually explicit continual memory-consolidation paradigm for LMs, which looks like a genuinely new direction.
items.primary_topic_label: Memory Structures and Agent Memory Systems
items.spotlight_comment: The paper proposes an unusually explicit continual memory-consolidation paradigm for LMs, which looks like a genuinely new direction.

items.title: Echo: KV-Cache-Free Associative Recall with Spectral Koopman Operators
items.summary: The KV-cache-free associative recall mechanism looks like a genuinely new memory architecture direction beyond standard transformer caching or plain SSM recurrence.
items.primary_topic_label: Memory Structures and Agent Memory Systems
items.spotlight_comment: The KV-cache-free associative recall mechanism looks like a genuinely new memory architecture direction beyond standard transformer caching or plain SSM recurrence.

items.title: Beyond Linear Attention: Softmax Transformers Implement In-Context Reinforcement Learning
items.summary: This opens a new theoretical bridge between standard Transformer forward passes and in-context reinforcement learning under realistic softmax attention.
items.spotlight_comment: This opens a new theoretical bridge between standard Transformer forward passes and in-context reinforcement learning under realistic softmax attention.

paper_spotlight.label: Daily Hot Papers
paper_spotlight.description: Papers that feel broadly important to the day and belong in the hotspot paper feed.

items.title: When Are Experts Misrouted? Counterfactual Routing Analysis in Mixture-of-Experts Language Models
items.summary: MoE routing in widely used LMs is a broadly important current topic, and the paper offers a concrete mechanistic diagnosis plus actionable improvement.
items.spotlight_comment: MoE routing in widely used LMs is a broadly important current topic, and the paper offers a concrete mechanistic diagnosis plus actionable improvement.

items.title: Sparse Attention as a Range Searching Problem: Towards an Inference-Efficient Index for KV Cache
items.summary: Broadly important today: Recasts sparse attention retrieval as halfspace range searching and proposes a KV-cache index with zero-false-negative guarantees.
items.spotlight_comment: Broadly important today: Recasts sparse attention retrieval as halfspace range searching and proposes a KV-cache index with zero-false-negative guarantees.

items.title: Reformulating KV Cache Eviction Problem for Long-Context LLM Inference
items.summary: Long-context inference efficiency is a central active issue, and this paper proposes a materially different cache eviction formulation.
items.spotlight_comment: Long-context inference efficiency is a central active issue, and this paper proposes a materially different cache eviction formulation.

items.title: RateQuant: Optimal Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization via Rate-Distortion Theory
items.summary: Broadly important today: Uses rate-distortion theory to learn per-head mixed-precision KV-cache allocations, avoiding distortion-model mismatch.
items.spotlight_comment: Broadly important today: Uses rate-distortion theory to learn per-head mixed-precision KV-cache allocations, avoiding distortion-model mismatch.

items.title: Memory-Efficient Looped Transformer: Decoupling Compute from Memory in Looped Language Models
items.summary: Broadly important today: Introduces a shared-KV recurrent transformer that keeps reasoning memory constant across loops instead of growing with iterative depth.
items.spotlight_comment: Broadly important today: Introduces a shared-KV recurrent transformer that keeps reasoning memory constant across loops instead of growing with iterative depth.

items.title: LKV: End-to-End Learning of Head-wise Budgets and Token Selection for LLM KV Cache Eviction
items.summary: Broadly important today: Learns head-wise budgets and token eviction for KV-cache compression end-to-end instead of relying on heuristic cache policies.
items.spotlight_comment: Broadly important today: Learns head-wise budgets and token eviction for KV-cache compression end-to-end instead of relying on heuristic cache policies.
